summary: I was born and raised on a farm in Lithonia, Georgia, back in the early 1900s. My family worked the land there, and I spent my childhood working on farms around Lithonia, learning to drill rock in the local quarries. It was a simple life, and although I never got a bicycle, I had a mule to ride which I thought was better. Lithonia is where my roots are, and it shaped much of who I am.

I have a sister who lives in Cincinnati. While I stayed mostly in the South and worked in places like West Virginia and Virginia, my family spread out a bit, and Cincinnati was one place my sister settled. Though I didn't spend much time there myself, it’s part of my family’s story.

Another one of my sisters lives in Decatur, Georgia. Like my sister in Cincinnati, she’s doing well and married. Decatur is not far from where I grew up, but I didn’t spend much time there myself. It’s good to know family is doing well there.

When I was about twenty-four, I went to North Carolina to work at the Big Rock quarry. I only stayed there about six months, but I made good money drilling rock—five twenty a day, which was better than what I made before. It was hard work but a good experience that helped me learn the trade.

The Big Rock quarry in North Carolina was where I worked drilling rock when I was about twenty-four years old. I learned a lot there and earned five twenty a day, which was pretty good pay at the time. It was tough work, but it gave me skills I used later on in other places.

I worked in Atlanta during the winters when I was younger, driving teams and sometimes getting into trouble for shooting craps or eyeballing games on Saturday nights. Atlanta was a busy place, and I learned a lot about work and life there. Later, after the war, I stayed around Atlanta for a while before moving on to other jobs.

I spent a good part of my working life in West Virginia, working in the coal mines and later selling produce with a truck. After my accident in Virginia, I lived with cousins in West Virginia and managed to make a living for a while. It’s where I felt I could get money easier and where I planned to return if my health improved.

In 1918, I was drafted into the army and sent to Camp Lee. I didn’t see any fighting but stayed at the camp with a company made up of colored men and white officers. I wanted to fight, but the war ended before I got the chance.

After the war ended, they sent me to Camp Gordon before I was discharged. I stayed around there for a while before moving on to work in Virginia. Camp Gordon was part of my transition back to civilian life after the army.

I worked loading coal at the V.I.C. mines in Tom Creek, Virginia, for about four years. That’s where I had the accident that cost me both my legs above the knees. It was a hard time, and after seven months in the hospital, I ended up living with relatives because I couldn’t work anymore.

The V.I.C. mines in Tom Creek, Virginia, were where I worked loading coal before my accident. It was tough, physical labor, and I was proud of the work I did there. Unfortunately, that’s also where I lost my legs in a tragic accident involving the mine cars.

The American Legion helped me get a government pension of forty dollars a month after my accident. That money helped me pay board and live with my family. Later, when Roosevelt cut my pension to thirty dollars, I hoped the Republicans would get back in so I could get my full amount again.

When Franklin D. Roosevelt became president, he cut my government pension from forty dollars to thirty dollars a month. That was a hard blow for me because I relied on that money to live. I hoped the Republicans would return to power so I could get my full pension back.

I’ve always voted for the Republican Party when I could, including for Hoover and Alf Landon. I’m hoping they get back in office so I can get my pension increased again. If things don’t change, I might not vote next year. I believe in working hard and don’t like government handouts unless you really need them.

I voted for Herbert Hoover when he ran for president. I supported him because I believed in the Republican way of doing things. Though times got tough, I stuck with my political beliefs and hoped for better days.

I also voted for Alf Landon during his run for president. Like with Hoover, I believed he represented the values I stood for. I’ve been consistent in my voting for Republicans, hoping they bring back better policies.

I’ve had stomach trouble for about ten years and have been thinking about going to Forty Light Hospital to see if they can help me. I keep putting it off, but I know I should get some treatment there. It’s one of the few places I might find some relief.

After my accident, I lived with my cousin Doll Hawk in Lynch, Kentucky, for about two years. It was a place where I could get some support while I adjusted to life without my legs. Later, I moved back to West Virginia to live with another cousin.
